volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 4.4.738 - Section 4.7.824b)

sections.label: Section 4.4.738
sections.original: Atque † idem hercle em perdundum magis quam ad scribundumst cito .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , that hand is more active by far at lavishing than at writing .

sections.label: Section 4.4.739
sections.original: Loquere . hoc scriptumst .
sections.translation: Say on ; that ’ s written down .

sections.label: Section 4.4.739b
sections.original: “ Nunc , pater mi , proin tu ab eo ut caveas tibi ,
sections.translation: Now , my dear father , do you henceforth be on your guard against him ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.740
sections.original: sycophantias componit , aurum ut abs ted auferat ;
sections.translation: for he is forming knavish plans to deprive you of the gold , and has declared that he will have it beyond a doubt . Write legibly .

sections.label: Section 4.4.742
sections.original: Dic modo .
sections.translation: You only dictate .

sections.label: Section 4.4.742b
sections.original: “ Atque id pollicetur se daturum aurum mihi ,
sections.translation: And he promises that he will give this gold to me , for me to give away to naughty women , and to consume it and live like a Greek [*] in dens of infamy . But , father , do you take care that he does not impose on you this day ; I entreat you , do beware .

sections.label: Section 4.4.745
sections.original: Loquere porro .
sections.translation: Only say on .

sections.label: Section 4.4.745b
sections.original: Adscribe dum etiam .
sections.translation: Just write on , then .

sections.label: Section 4.4.745c
sections.original: Loquere quid scribam modo .
sections.translation: Only say what I am to write

sections.label: Section 4.4.746
sections.original: “ Sed , pater , quod promisisti mihi , te quaeso ut memineris ,
sections.translation: But , father , what you promised me I beg that you will remember , that you will not beat him , but keep him in chains at your house at home . Do you give me the wax and cord forthwith . Come , fasten it , and seal it in an instant .

sections.label: Section 4.4.749
sections.original: Obsecro , quid istís ad istunc usust conscriptis modum ,
sections.translation: Prithee , what use is there in this writing after such a fashion ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.750
sections.original: ut tibi ne quid credat atque ut vinctum te adservet domi ?
sections.translation: that he is to give no credence to you , and to keep you in chains at home ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.751
sections.original: Quia mi ita lubet . potin ut cures te atque ut ne parcas mihi ?
sections.translation: Because it pleases me so . Can ’ t you possibly take care of your own self , and have no thought about me ? In confidence in myself I undertook the task , and at my own peril do I carry on the matter .

sections.label: Section 4.4.753
sections.original: Aequom dicis .
sections.translation: You say what ’ s true .

sections.label: Section 4.4.753b
sections.original: Cedo tabellas .
sections.translation: Give me the tablets .

sections.label: Section 4.4.753c
sections.original: Accipe .
sections.translation: Take them .

sections.label: Section 4.4.753d
sections.original: Animum advortite .
sections.translation: Give attention now ; Mnesilochus , and you , Pistoclerus , do you take care that each of you goes at once and reclines on his couch

sections.label: Section 4.4.755
sections.original: cum amica sua uterque accubitum eatis , ita negotiumst ,
sections.translation: with his mistress ; so ’ tis requisite ; and on that same spot where the couches are now laid , do you forthwith commence to carouse .

sections.label: Section 4.4.757
sections.original: Numquid aliud ?
sections.translation: Anything else ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.757b
sections.original: Hoc , atque etiam : úbi erit accubitum semel ,
sections.translation: This , and this especially : when you shall have once taken your places together , don ’ t you arise anyhow , until the signal shall be given by me .

sections.label: Section 4.4.759
sections.original: O imperatorem probum !
sections.translation: O skilful commander !

sections.label: Section 4.4.759b
sections.original: Iam bis bibisse oportuit .
sections.translation: You ought by this time to have taken your second draught .

sections.label: Section 4.4.760
sections.original: Fugimus .—
sections.translation: Capital ; let ’ s go .

sections.label: Section 4.4.760b
sections.original: Vos vostrum curate officium , ego efficiam meum .
sections.translation: Do you take care of your duty , and I ’ ll do mine .

sections.label: Section 4.5.761
sections.original: insánum magnum molior negotium ,
sections.translation: A tremendously great business am I undertaking , and I have my fears how in this one day I may accomplish it . But now I have occasion for the old blade to be fierce and savage at me . For it suits not this plan of mine

sections.label: Section 4.5.765
sections.original: senem tranquillum ésse ubi me aspexerit .
sections.translation: that the old fellow should be calm when he has beheld me in his presence . I ’ ll turn him about [*] this day , finely , if I live . I ’ ll have him parched as well as ever pea was parched [*] . I ’ ll walk before the door , that when he comes out , at once as he comes up , I may put the letters [*] in his hand .

sections.label: Section 4.6.770
sections.original: Nimium illaec res est magnae dividiae mihi ,
sections.translation: This is a matter of great vexation to me , that Chrysalus has thus escaped me this day .

sections.label: Section 4.6.772
sections.original: Salvos sum , iratus est senex . nunc est mihi
sections.translation: I ’ m all right : the old fellow is in a passion . Now ’ s my time for me to accost my man .

sections.label: Section 4.6.773b
sections.original: Quis loquitur prope ?
sections.translation: Who speaks hard by ? ( Looking round .) Why surely this is Chrysalus , I think .

sections.label: Section 4.6.774b
sections.original: Accessero .
sections.translation: I ’ ll accost him .

sections.label: Section 4.6.775
sections.original: Bone serve , salve . quid fit ? quam mox navigo
sections.translation: My honest servant , hail to you . What ’ s doing now ? How soon do I set sail for Ephesus , to bring back the gold home from Theotimus ? What — silent ? I swear by all the Gods , if I didn ’ t love my son so much as to wish everything done for him that he desires ⋯

sections.label: Section 4.6.779_780
sections.original: ut tua iam virgis latera lacerentur probe
sections.translation: that your sides should be wealed now right well with rods , and that in irons at the mill you should be lingering out your life . I ’ ve learnt of Mnesilochus all your wicked pranks .

sections.label: Section 4.6.783
sections.original: Men criminatust ? optimest : ego sum malus ,
sections.translation: Has he accused me ? ’ Tis very good ; I am a bad one , I am a cursed one — a wicked one . Only reflect upon the matter . I ’ ll utter not one word .

sections.label: Section 4.6.785b
sections.original: Etiam , carnufex ,
sections.translation: What ? Hangdog , do you even threaten me [*] ?

sections.label: Section 4.6.789
sections.original: Cedo .
sections.translation: Give it me .

sections.label: Section 4.6.789b
sections.original: Nosce signum .
sections.translation: Take notice of the seal .

sections.label: Section 4.6.789c
sections.original: Novi . ubi ipse est ?
sections.translation: I know it . Where is he himself ?

sections.label: Section 4.6.789d
sections.original: Nescio .
sections.translation: I don ’ t know .

sections.label: Section 4.6.790
sections.original: nil iam me oportet scire . oblitus sum omnia .
sections.translation: I ought to know nothing now ; I ’ ve forgotten everything . I know that I ’ m a slave ; I know not even that which I do know . ( Aside .) Now from the springe this thrush is catching at the worm ; he ’ ll be finely noosed this day , so well I ’ ve set the snare .

sections.label: Section 4.6.794
sections.original: Mane dum parumper ; iam exeo ad te , Chrysale .—
sections.translation: Stay here but a moment ; I ’ ll return to you directly , Chrysalus .

sections.label: Section 4.6.795
sections.original: Vt verba mihi dat , ut nescio quam rem gerat .
sections.translation: How he does dissemble with me ; how ignorant I am of the business he ’ s about . He has gone to fetch slaves from in-doors to bind me . The ship speeds prosperously on ; finely , too , is this craft boarding it [*] . But I ’ ll hold my tongue , for I hear the door opening .

sections.label: Section 4.7.799
sections.original: Constringe tu illi , Artamo , actutum manus .
sections.translation: Artamo , do you fasten the hands of that fellow this very instant .

sections.label: Section 4.7.800
sections.original: Quid feci ?
sections.translation: What have I done ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.800b
sections.original: Impinge pugnum , si muttiverit .
sections.translation: Pitch your fist into him if he mutters a word . ( To CHRYSALUS , pointing at the tablets .) What does this letter say ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.801b
sections.original: Quid me rogas ?
sections.translation: Why do you ask me ? As I received it from him , so I ’ ve brought it sealed to you .

sections.label: Section 4.7.803
sections.original: Eho tu , ⋯ loquitatusne es gnato meo
sections.translation: Come now , you rascal , have you not shamefully abused my son in your talk , because he gave me up that gold ,

sections.label: Section 4.7.805
sections.original: et te dixisti id aurum ablaturum tamen
sections.translation: and said that you would still take that gold away from me by some knavish trick ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.806b
sections.original: Egone istuc dixi ?
sections.translation: Have I said so ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.806c
sections.original: Ita .
sections.translation: You have .

sections.label: Section 4.7.807
sections.original: Quis homost qui dicat me dixisse istuc ?
sections.translation: What person is there , who says that I ’ ve said so ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.807b
sections.original: Tace ,
sections.translation: Hold your tongue . No person says so ; this letter which you have brought me accuses you . See , ’ tis this that requests you to be chained .

sections.label: Section 4.7.810
sections.original: Aha , Bellorophontem tuos me fecit filius :
sections.translation: Ah ! your son has been making a Bellerophon [*] of me ; I myself brought this letter , for the purpose that I might be bound . Be it so .

sections.label: Section 4.7.812
sections.original: Propterea hoc facio , ut suadeas gnato meo
sections.translation: This I am doing for this reason , because you persuade my son to live like a Greek with you , you thrice-dotted villain .

sections.label: Section 4.7.814
sections.original: O stulte , stulte , nescis nunc venire te ;
sections.translation: O fool , fool , you know not that you are at this moment on sale ;

sections.label: Section 4.7.815
sections.original: atque in eopse adstas lapide , ut praeco praedicat .
sections.translation: and that you are standing on the very stone [*] as the auctioneer puts you up .

sections.label: Section 4.7.816
sections.original: Responde : quis me vendit ?
sections.translation: Answer me ; who is selling me ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.816b
sections.original: Quem di diligunt
sections.translation: He whom the Gods favour [*] dies in youth , while he is in his health , has his senses and judgment sound . This person ( pointing to NICOBULUS ) , if any God had favoured him , ought to have been dead more than ten years — aye , more than twenty years ago .

sections.label: Section 4.7.820
sections.original: terrai ódium ámbulat , iam nil sapit
sections.translation: ’ Tis for long , he has walked , a nuisance , on the earth ; so devoid is he of either judgment or sense . He is of as much value as a rotten mushroom is .

sections.label: Section 4.7.822
sections.original: Tun terrae me odium esse autumas ? abducite hunc
sections.translation: Do you think that I am a nuisance to the earth ? Away with him in-doors , and tie him tightly to the post . You shall never take away any gold from here .

sections.label: Section 4.7.824b
sections.original: Át qui iam dabis .
sections.translation: No , but you ’ ll soon be giving it me .
